questions.question: Why does Next.js create hundreds of Prisma Client instances during development without the global singleton pattern, and what exact symptom does this cause?
options: Without the singleton, Prisma runs in read-only mode during development.

Next.js uses Hot Module Replacement (HMR) to apply code changes without a full server restart. Each time you save a file, the affected modules are re-evaluated. If lib/prisma.ts runs new PrismaClient() at module level without the global singleton, each HMR cycle creates a new PrismaClient instance with its own connection pool (typically 10 connections). After 10-15 saves, you hit PostgreSQL's max_connections limit (default 100). Symptom: 'too many connections' error from PostgreSQL — your API endpoints start returning 500 errors until you restart the dev server.
Without the singleton, Prisma uses SQLite instead of PostgreSQL in development.
HMR causes queries to be duplicated — results are returned twice without the singleton.
questions.answer: Next.js uses Hot Module Replacement (HMR) to apply code changes without a full server restart. Each time you save a file, the affected modules are re-evaluated. If lib/prisma.ts runs new PrismaClient() at module level without the global singleton, each HMR cycle creates a new PrismaClient instance with its own connection pool (typically 10 connections). After 10-15 saves, you hit PostgreSQL's max_connections limit (default 100). Symptom: 'too many connections' error from PostgreSQL — your API endpoints start returning 500 errors until you restart the dev server.
questions.explanation: The fix works because globalThis survives HMR — it's not reset between cycles. globalThis.prisma ?? new PrismaClient() returns the existing instance on subsequent evaluations. This is only needed in development; in production, Next.js never hot-reloads, so each serverless function instance creates exactly one PrismaClient in its lifecycle.

questions.question: Prisma Client cannot run on Vercel Edge Runtime (Edge Functions, Middleware). What is the fundamental reason, and what is Prisma's solution?
options: Edge Runtime doesn't support TypeScript which Prisma requires.

Prisma Client requires Node.js-specific APIs (net.createConnection, tcp sockets) to establish a direct TCP connection to PostgreSQL. Edge Runtime (Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge) is a stripped-down V8 environment with NO Node.js APIs — it only has fetch, WebCrypto, and Web APIs. Direct TCP from Edge is impossible. Prisma Accelerate is the solution: it runs a global connection pooler as a cloud service reachable via HTTPS/fetch. Prisma Client communicates with Accelerate over HTTP instead of raw TCP, making it compatible with any fetch-capable environment including Edge Runtime.
Edge Runtime prohibits database connections because edge functions run on CDN nodes.
Prisma requires a UNIX socket connection which is unavailable on Edge Runtime.
questions.answer: Prisma Client requires Node.js-specific APIs (net.createConnection, tcp sockets) to establish a direct TCP connection to PostgreSQL. Edge Runtime (Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge) is a stripped-down V8 environment with NO Node.js APIs — it only has fetch, WebCrypto, and Web APIs. Direct TCP from Edge is impossible. Prisma Accelerate is the solution: it runs a global connection pooler as a cloud service reachable via HTTPS/fetch. Prisma Client communicates with Accelerate over HTTP instead of raw TCP, making it compatible with any fetch-capable environment including Edge Runtime.
questions.explanation: Alternative to Prisma Accelerate for Edge: use a Supabase client (communicates via REST/HTTP), or PlanetScale serverless driver (HTTP-based MySQL), or Neon serverless driver (HTTP-based PostgreSQL). These are all HTTP-native database drivers designed specifically for Edge environments.

questions.question: What is the stale-while-revalidate (swr) cache strategy in Prisma Accelerate, and how does it improve perceived performance?
options: SWR makes Prisma only cache writes, not reads — reads always go to the database.

With ttl=60 only: data is served from cache for 60s, then the next request is slow (waits for fresh data from DB). With swr=30 added (on top of ttl): after the ttl expires, serve the STALE data immediately to the user (still fast!) while simultaneously fetching fresh data in the background for future requests. Users never see a slow response — only ever cache hits or stale-but-fast data. The background fetch updates the cache without blocking any user. This creates a 'always fast, eventually consistent' experience.
SWR (stale-while-revalidate) prevents caching of queries that include WHERE clauses.
SWR in Prisma Accelerate is identical to the swr option in React Query — they share the same mechanism.
questions.answer: With ttl=60 only: data is served from cache for 60s, then the next request is slow (waits for fresh data from DB). With swr=30 added (on top of ttl): after the ttl expires, serve the STALE data immediately to the user (still fast!) while simultaneously fetching fresh data in the background for future requests. Users never see a slow response — only ever cache hits or stale-but-fast data. The background fetch updates the cache without blocking any user. This creates a 'always fast, eventually consistent' experience.
questions.explanation: SWR timing example: cache set at t=0. At t=60 (ttl expired): next request gets stale data (instant) + background DB query starts. At t=60+200ms: background query completes, cache updated. Requests at t=61 to t=90 (swr window): get refreshed data. At t=91 (ttl+swr exceeded): next request triggers another background refresh. Ideal for: product listings, user profiles, any data where millisecond-perfect freshness isn't required.
